Paper name: An Explainable Framework for Detecting and Mitigating Prompt Injection in Chatbot and RAG Systems
Method: Lightweight guardrail middleware with multi-class prompt classification (TF-IDF+Logistic Regression or DistilBERT+LoRA), policy-driven action recommendation, and explainable risk attribution (SHAP for TF-IDF, Integrated Gradients for neural models)
Pros: Lightweight, near-zero cost, reproducible within 15 days, explainable AI components, multi-class classification covering 5 attack types, policy-driven mitigation
Cons: May require retraining for new attack variants, performance depends on quality and diversity of training data, limited to text-based prompts
Dataset: Custom dataset curated from OWASP Prompt Injection Cheat Sheet covering benign, injection-direct, injection-indirect, data-exfiltration, and tool-misuse categories
Metric: Accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score for each class; robustness measured via paraphrasing, multilingual (Vietnamese/English), and noise perturbation tests
Result: Effective detection and mitigation of prompt injection attacks with explainable insights; deployable as FastAPI endpoint with Gradio demo
Conclusion: Effective prompt-injection defense is accessible without requiring large-scale infrastructure, demonstrating that lightweight solutions can provide strong security guarantees

Paper name: Defending Against Indirect Prompt Injection Attacks With Spotlighting
Method: Spotlighting family of prompt engineering techniques to help LLMs distinguish between system instructions and injected instructions
Pros: Minimal impact on NLP tasks, reduces attack success rate from >50% to <2%, simple to implement
Cons: Primarily focused on indirect prompt injection, may not cover all attack types
Dataset: Custom corpus of documents with indirect prompt injection attacks
Result: Spotlighting reduces attack success rate from >50% to <2% with minimal impact on task performance
Conclusion: Spotlighting provides robust defense against indirect prompt injection with minimal detrimental impact to underlying NLP tasks

Paper name: Defense Against Prompt Injection Attack by Leveraging Attack Techniques
Method: Inverting prompt injection attack techniques to create defense methods by repeating attack process with original input instruction
Pros: Training-free, leverages existing attack techniques for defense, outperforms existing training-free defenses
Cons: May not generalize to all attack variants, effectiveness depends on similarity between attack and defense techniques
Result: Defense techniques outperform existing training-free defense approaches
Conclusion: Prompt injection attack techniques can be leveraged for defensive purposes by inverting their intention

Method: CaMeL (Capabilities and Policies for Language Models) - creates protective system layer that extracts control and data flows from trusted queries
Pros: Provides provable security, prevents data exfiltration, secures LLM even when underlying model is susceptible
Cons: May reduce task performance (77% vs 84% task completion in AgentDojo), requires system-level integration
Dataset: AgentDojo benchmark for LLM agent security
Metric: Task completion rate with provable security
Result: CaMeL solves 77% of tasks with provable security (vs 84% with undefended system) in AgentDojo
Conclusion: CaMeL provides robust defense against prompt injection by design, creating a protective layer around LLMs

Paper name: SecInfer: Preventing Prompt Injection via Inference-time Scaling
Method: Inference-time scaling with system-prompt-guided sampling and target-task-guided aggregation
Pros: Effective against existing and adaptive attacks, leverages additional compute at inference, no model retraining needed
Cons: Increased computational cost at inference, may increase latency
Result: Effectively mitigates both existing and adaptive prompt injection attacks
Conclusion: SecInfer provides effective defense against prompt injection by leveraging inference-time scaling

Paper name: Defending Against Prompt Injection With a Few DefensiveTokens
Method: DefensiveTokens - special tokens with embeddings optimized for security, inserted before LLM input
Pros: Test-time defense comparable to training-time approaches, flexible utility-security trade-off, minimal utility drop
Cons: Requires token embedding optimization, may not work for all model architectures
Loss function: Defensive loss for token embedding optimization
Result: DefensiveTokens achieve security comparable to training-time defenses with minimal utility drop
Conclusion: DefensiveTokens enable flexible switching between state-of-the-art utility and near-state-of-the-art security at test time

Paper name: PROMPTARMOR: AN ESSENTIAL BASELINE FOR PROMPT INJECTION DEFENSES
Method: Prompting off-the-shelf LLM with strong reasoning capabilities to detect and remove injected prompts
Pros: Lightweight, easy to deploy, strong baseline for evaluating defenses, leverages modern LLM reasoning
Cons: Effectiveness depends on underlying LLM reasoning capabilities, may have false positives/negatives
Metric: False positive rate, false negative rate
Result: PromptArmor using GPT-4o achieves low false positive and false negative rates
Conclusion: Prompting strong LLMs should be reconsidered as important baseline for evaluating prompt injection defenses

Paper name: A Multi-Agent LLM Defense Pipeline Against Prompt Injection Attacks
Method: Multi-agent pipeline with specialized agents for detection, isolation, and mitigation of prompt injections
Pros: Comprehensive defense strategy, leverages agent specialization, can handle various attack types
Cons: Increased system complexity, potential latency from inter-agent communication
Loss function: Varies by agent (typically cross-entropy for classification agents)
Metric: Detection accuracy, mitigation effectiveness, system robustness
Result: Effective detection and mitigation of diverse prompt injection attacks through agent collaboration
Conclusion: Multi-agent approach provides robust defense against prompt injection by leveraging specialized agent capabilities

Paper name: Formalizing and Benchmarking Prompt Injection Attacks and Defenses
Method: Framework to formalize PI attacks and defenses, systematic evaluation on 5 attacks and 10 defenses with 10 LLMs and 7 tasks
Pros: Provides common benchmark for quantitative evaluation, bridges gap between case studies and systematic understanding
Cons: Focuses on evaluation framework rather than proposing new defense technique
Loss function: Varies by defense method (cross-entropy for classification-based defenses)
Dataset: Custom benchmark covering 5 PI attack types and 7 downstream tasks
Metric: Attack Success Rate (ASR), Benign Answering Rate (BAR)
Result: Systematic evaluation of 10 defenses showing varying effectiveness across attack types and tasks
Conclusion: Provides common benchmark and framework for future PI attack and defense research

Paper name: Can Indirect Prompt Injection Attacks Be Detected and Removed?
Method: Framework to formalize PI attacks and defenses, systematic evaluation on 5 attacks and 10 defenses with 10 LLMs and 7 tasks; Code available at https://github.com/liu00222/Open-Prompt-Injection
Pros: Addresses indirect PI specifically, provides both detection and removal mechanisms, constructs benchmark dataset
Cons: May require training custom detection/removal models, focused on indirect PI only
Dataset: Crafted benchmark dataset for indirect PI attack evaluation
Metric: Detection accuracy, removal effectiveness, Attack Success Rate (ASR)
Result: Shows feasibility of detecting and removing indirect PI attacks with proposed methods
Conclusion: Indirect prompt injection attacks can be effectively detected and removed using segmentation/extraction methods

Paper name: Defending against Indirect Prompt Injection by Instruction Detection
Method: InstructDetector - leverages behavioral states of LLMs to identify potential IPI attacks; Code available at https://github.com/MYVAE/Instruction-detection
Pros: High detection accuracy (99.60% in-domain, 96.90% out-of-domain), reduces ASR to 0.03% on BIPIA benchmark
Cons: Requires access to model internal states/behavioral features, may not work for black-box APIs
Loss function: Binary cross-entropy for behavioral state classification
Dataset: BIPIA (Benchmark for Indirect Prompt Injection Attacks)
Result: InstructDetector achieves 99.60% in-domain and 96.90% out-of-domain detection accuracy
Conclusion: Leveraging LLM behavioral states enables highly effective detection of indirect prompt injection attacks

Paper name: PIGuard: Prompt Injection Guardrail via Mitigating Overdefense for Free
Method: Guardrail that mitigates overdefense tendencies in existing PI defenses while maintaining security
Pros: Addresses overdefense problem (excessive false positives), maintains security while reducing unnecessary blocking
Cons: Builds on existing defenses rather than proposing entirely new approach
Dataset: Various PI attack and benign prompt datasets
Metric: False positive rate, false negative rate, Attack Success Rate (ASR)
Result: Reduces both false negatives and false positives while maintaining low ASR
Conclusion: Mitigating overdefense improves practicality of PI guardrails without sacrificing security

Paper name: [Under Review] PROMPTARMOR: AN ESSENTIAL BASELINE FOR PROMPT INJECTION DEFENSES
Method: Prompting off-the-shelf LLM with strong reasoning to detect and remove injected prompts
Pros: Lightweight, easy-to-deploy, strong baseline for evaluation, leverages modern LLM reasoning
Cons: Effectiveness depends on underlying LLM reasoning, may have non-zero FPR/FNR
Dataset: AgentDojo, Open Prompt Injection, TensorTrust benchmarks
Metric: False positive rate, false negative rate
Result: PromptArmor achieves <1% FPR/FNR on AgentDojo and <5% on other benchmarks with strong LLMs
Conclusion: Prompting strong LLMs should be reconsidered as important baseline for PI defense evaluation

Paper name: PROMPTINJECT: A Prosaic Alignment Framework for Mask-Based Iterative Adversarial Prompt Composition
Method: Framework for studying goal hijacking and prompt leaking attacks
Pros: Early systematic study of prompt injection vulnerabilities, proposed two fundamental attack types
Cons: Focuses on attack formulation rather than defense, limited to GPT-3
Result: Introduced goal hijacking and prompt leaking as fundamental PI attack types
Conclusion: Foundational work that established prompt injection as a serious vulnerability in LLMs

Method: Query-based black-box attack that leverages API access to construct adversarial examples
Pros: More effective than transfer-only attacks, works in realistic black-box setting
Cons: Requires API access, focused on attack generation rather than defense
Result: Can cause GPT-3.5 to emit harmful strings that transfer attacks fail at
Conclusion: Advanced black-box attack method that improved understanding of PI attack capabilities

Paper name: Early Approaches to Adversarial Fine-Tuning for Prompt Injection Defense
Method: Adversarial fine-tuning with structured input delimiters
Pros: Empirical demonstration of correlation between model capability and attack vulnerability
Cons: Early approach, superseded by more advanced techniques
Result: Established principles like structured input separation that influenced modern defenses
Conclusion: Foundational defense research that contributed to modern PI defense techniques
